items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: Students see 'piano' and think of the instrument. As dynamic, p = soft (from Italian). The instrument is actually a shortened 'pianoforte' (soft-loud) because it could play at varying dynamics, unlike the harpsichord.

items.q: What is a tone row in serial music?
items.correct: An ordered series of all 12 chromatic pitches, used as the basis of a piece.
items.misconception: A row of notes played together as a chord.
items.random_wrong: Any sequence of notes a composer chooses.
items.why: Students miss the SPECIFIC structure. Schoenberg's 12-tone technique uses ALL 12 pitches in a fixed order before any repeats, generating melodies and harmonies from that row.

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.q: In sonata form, in what key does the second subject normally appear in the exposition of a piece in a major key?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dominant. “Tonic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which composer is credited with significantly expanding the coda in sonata-form movements?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Beethoven. “Mozart” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many main sections does classical sonata form contain (exposition, development, recapitulation)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 sections. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
